question: In LAMMPS, I plan to determine the melting temperature of elemental potassium (bcc) at ~1 atm using a two-phase (solid–liquid) coexistence simulation. Which validated potassium interatomic potential(s) should I use—preferably EAM/MEAM potentials available in the LAMMPS potentials directory—with exact file names and literature citations? For the recommended potential(s), what melting temperature at ambient pressure should I expect if the model is accurate (experimental Tm ≈ 336–337 K), and what Tm values have been reported in the original papers or subsequent validation studies?

Identify and compare published interatomic potentials for elemental potassium (K) that are validated for bcc K at 0 K and ~0 GPa. Specifically, which potential (EAM/MEAM/Finnis–Sinclair, etc.; give name/author/year/DOI or OpenKIM/NIST-IPR ID) best reproduces, simultaneously, the equilibrium lattice parameter a0, elastic constants C11, C12, C44 (and derived bulk/shear moduli), cohesive energy, and monovacancy formation energy? Include quantitative errors versus experiment/DFT and note LAMMPS compatibility; exclude models tuned for high-pressure phases or ionic/polarizable descriptions.

Seeking a potassium (K) interatomic potential for MD (LAMMPS/OpenKIM) that (1) explicitly stabilizes the bcc phase at low temperature/0 K (positive C′, no imaginary phonons) and (2) has been validated by accurately reproducing K’s melting behavior (e.g., correct ambient-pressure melting point ≈336.5 K via two-phase coexistence or Z-method). Which specific K potential (EAM/MEAM/Finnis–Sinclair/Gupta, etc.) meets these criteria? Please provide the potential name/file, primary citation, and a download/source link.

Identify the interatomic potential that, although fitted only to 0 K static crystalline properties (e.g., lattice parameter, cohesive energy, elastic constants) and without any liquid or finite-temperature training data, has been demonstrated to accurately predict finite-temperature thermodynamics such as melting points. Specify the potential’s name and family (e.g., EAM/MEAM, Tersoff/BOP, GAP, MTP), the material system(s) where this transferability was shown, and provide primary literature citations.

Locate the original publication that defines an Embedded-Atom Method Finnis–Sinclair (EAM/FS) potential for elemental potassium (K) and provides a LAMMPS-compatible K.eam.fs file for use with pair_style eam/fs. Return the full citation (authors, year, journal, DOI) and a reliable download link (e.g., LAMMPS/potentials, NIST Interatomic Potentials Repository, OpenKIM, or the authors’ site). Confirm the file is in eam/fs format (not eam/alloy or MEAM), note whether it is a single-element K potential or part of a multi-element set, and summarize the fitting/validation properties (e.g., bcc K lattice parameter, cohesive energy, elastic constants, vacancy/melting behavior) and applicable temperature/pressure range.

In LAMMPS using the OpenKIM interface (pair_style kim) for a multicomponent system with multiple atom types, I need a Lennard–Jones 12-6 nonbonded model that applies Lorentz–Berthelot (LB) mixing for cross terms and enforces an energy-shifted cutoff so the potential energy is continuous and zero at r = rc (force continuity not required). Which specific OpenKIM model/model driver and parameterization (e.g., “LJ 12-6 with LB mixing, energy-shifted cutoff”) should I select?

When using a universal shifted 12-6 Lennard-Jones pair potential (uSLJ, “K” parameter set) to compute the elastic constants (C11, C12, C44) of bulk bcc potassium at 0 K/ambient pressure, what fundamental limitation should you watch for—namely, that central pair potentials enforce the zero-pressure Cauchy relation (C12 = C44) and omit many-body metallic bonding—making them unable to reproduce K’s real elastic response and potentially mis-stabilizing bcc?

In OpenKIM, for the Dimer_Gas_Phase test using the generic LennardJones612 model driver to reproduce the gas-phase K2 equilibrium bond length and dissociation energy, which specific potassium LJ ParameterSet should be used? Please provide the exact OpenKIM ParameterSet name/ID for K (and its epsilon and sigma values or reference) that this test is intended to use.

Which LAMMPS interatomic potential and parameter file should I use to compare the 0 K (and pressure-dependent) stability of KCl in the B1 (rocksalt/NaCl) versus B2 (CsCl) structures? I need a validated alkali-halide model that encodes ionic radii via equilibrium pair distances (e.g., Tosi–Fumi/Born–Mayer–Huggins Buckingham + long-range Coulomb). Please recommend a concrete KCl parameter set or OpenKIM model (with citation), specify the appropriate pair_style (buck/coul/long or born/coul/long), and note required settings (charges K+=+1, Cl−=−1; Ewald/PPPM) known to reproduce the B1 ground state and reasonable B1→B2 transition pressure.

Identify a curated repository of calibrated interionic potentials for alkali halides (KI, KBr) that provides parameter sets (e.g., Buckingham/Born–Mayer–Huggins/Tosi–Fumi) and benchmarks for B1 (NaCl/rocksalt), B2 (CsCl), and B3 (zinc blende) structures, including energy–volume curves and relative phase stability. Which database (e.g., OpenKIM/NIST Interatomic Potentials Repository, JARVIS-FF) is specifically designed for such B1/B2/B3 studies?

Identify a LAMMPS-compatible machine-learning interatomic potential for molten LiCl–KCl mixtures that was trained on DFT with the SCAN meta-GGA (not PBE). Provide the model name/type (e.g., DeePMD, NequIP, GAP), citation/DOI and any GitHub/model repository, the composition and temperature ranges it was trained/validated on (including the 58:42 mol% eutectic and off-eutectic compositions), and how long-range electrostatics/polarization are treated. Summarize the experimental validations reported—X-ray/neutron structure factors and RDFs, density vs temperature, viscosity, ionic self-diffusion coefficients for Li+, K+, and Cl−, and electrical conductivity—along with quantitative errors versus experiment (e.g., RMSE or percent errors). Include brief LAMMPS setup details (pair_style, required plugins, units) or a minimal input snippet for running the model.

For the published Gaussian Approximation Potential (GAP) for molten LiCl–KCl, what LiCl mole-fraction range was actually included in the training data? Specifically, were pure LiCl and KCl‑rich (very low LiCl) configurations present, or are those composition extremes absent—meaning simulations at x_LiCl near 0 or 1 would be extrapolations and thus unreliable?

Identify the specific Morse-based interatomic potential/model (e.g., modified/extended/generalized Morse pair potential) used in a classical study of body-centered-cubic potassium (K) that computed the equation of state and second-order elastic constants under arbitrary (finite) homogeneous deformation, and reported agreement with experimental data while satisfying the Born mechanical stability criteria (C11 − C12 > 0, C44 > 0, C11 + 2C12 > 0). Provide the model name and the primary bibliographic citation (authors, year, journal).

Identify an interatomic potential/force field with proven composition transferability for molten LiCl–KCl: specifically, a model trained without the eutectic composition that nevertheless reproduces the experimentally measured liquid structure of the LiCl–KCl eutectic (≈58:42 mol% LiCl:KCl) near its melting temperature, as validated by neutron/X-ray structure factors S(q) and partial RDFs gij(r). Compare classical rigid-ion and polarizable-ion models (e.g., Tosi–Fumi, Born–Mayer–Huggins, PIM/Drude) against machine-learning potentials (e.g., GAP, MTP, SNAP, NequIP/Allegro), and name the specific potential/parameter set with citations demonstrating this zero-shot composition transfer.

Which publication demonstrates constructing a simple analytic interatomic pair potential for bcc metallic potassium (K) using only three experimental inputs—cohesive energy (heat of vaporization), equilibrium lattice constant, and bulk modulus (compressibility)? Include approaches based on the universal binding energy relation/universal equation of state (Rose–Ferrante–Smith) that fit a Morse-like potential from E_coh, a0, and B0.
